claims.text: Cross-domain reinforcement learning (CDRL) is meant to improve the data efficiency of RL by leveraging the data samples collected from a source domain to facilitate the learning in a similar target domain.
claims.source_quote: Cross-domain reinforcement learning (CDRL) is meant to improve the data efficiency of RL by leveraging the data samples collected from a source domain to facilitate the learning in a similar target domain.

claims.text: Cross-domain transfer in RL has two fundamental and intertwined challenges.
claims.source_quote: Despite its potential, cross-domain transfer in RL is known to have two fundamental and intertwined challenges

claims.text: The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space, making direct transfer infeasible.
claims.source_quote: The source and target domains can have distinct state space or action space, and this makes direct transfer infeasible

claims.text: Distinct state space or action space between source and target domains requires more sophisticated inter-domain mappings.
claims.source_quote: this makes direct transfer infeasible and thereby requires more sophisticated inter-domain mappings

claims.text: The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori.
claims.source_quote: The transferability of a source-domain model in RL is not easily identifiable a priori

claims.text: CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer.
claims.source_quote: hence CDRL can be prone to negative effect during transfer

claims.text: The paper proposes to jointly tackle the two challenges through the lens of cross-domain Bellman consistency and hybrid critic.
claims.source_quote: we propose to jointly tackle these two challenges through the lens of \textit{cross-domain Bellman consistency} and \textit{hybrid critic}

claims.text: The paper introduces the notion of cross-domain Bellman consistency as a way to measure transferability of a source-domain model.
claims.source_quote: we first introduce the notion of cross-domain Bellman consistency as a way to measure transferability of a source-domain model

claims.text: QAvatar combines the Q functions from both the source and target domains with an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function.
claims.source_quote: we propose $Q$Avatar, which combines the Q functions from both the source and target domains with an adaptive hyperparameter-free weight function

claims.text: The paper characterizes the convergence behavior of QAvatar.
claims.source_quote: Through this design, we characterize the convergence behavior of $Q$Avatar

claims.text: QAvatar achieves reliable transfer in the sense that it effectively leverages a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain.
claims.source_quote: show that $Q$Avatar achieves reliable transfer in the sense that it effectively leverages a source-domain Q function for knowledge transfer to the target domain

claims.text: QAvatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks, including locomotion and robot arm manipulation.
claims.source_quote: we demonstrate that $Q$Avatar achieves favorable transferability across various RL benchmark tasks, including locomotion and robot arm manipulation
